sourceFiles.name: thermal-conflict

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A simple thermal control model defining when a system should shut down and when a cooling fan should activate based on temperature.
- Operational domain: Likely industrial/electromechanical control or embedded system thermal management.
- Model scope: Small fragment containing two requirements and a minimal structural definition for a cooling subsystem and parent system.
- Known scope limitations: No behavior models, interfaces, allocations, verification cases, packages beyond one namespace, or decomposition beyond a single contained part. Checks involving those elements are assessed only for what is present and otherwise passed due to absence of duplicate instances.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  Two requirement definitions were examined and their texts express different thresholds and different actions, so they are not duplicates or near-duplicates.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  The model contains only one `part def CoolingSystem` and one `part def System`, with no parallel definitions, aliases, or repeated interface/item definitions.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or specialization relationships are present, so no duplicate relationships were found.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, action definitions, sequences, or message exchange models are present, so there is no duplicated behavior content to assess.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints  -  The attributes on `CoolingSystem` are distinct properties (`temperatureC`, `emergencyShutdownThresholdC`, `coolingFanStartThresholdC`) and are not repeated on usages or elsewhere in the model.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  The decomposition is minimal (`System` contains one `CoolingSystem` part), but there is no evidence of a redundant pass-through hierarchy, empty package, or single-step action decomposition.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated model content was identified, so there is no redundancy requiring justification as intentional architectural redundancy.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| None | 1.0 | HIGH | No redundancy issues identified in the provided model fragment. |

Overall Assessment Score: 1.0
Model Status: Good - minor improvements only
